PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION
The originaleditionof thisbook was publishedin 1916. At
that time only two Compensation caseshad been decidedby
the Court of Appeals . It was thereforenecessary to annotate
that editionwith Compensation cases from other states.
From 1916 to the presenttime, 1927, there have been pub-
lished52 volumes of the Kentucky Reports- Volumes 168 to
220. These reportscontaina greatwealth ofCompensation Law .
In addition to these cases, the Compensation Board has
compiled 6 volumes of Leading Decisions. Allof the Compensa-
tioncasesintheKentucky Reportsand intheLeadingDecisions
of the Board , have been carefullyread and analyzed, and the
points in each case have been extractedto form the annotations
in this Second Edition .
After completingthe annotationsfrom Kentucky cases,
referencesare made at the end of each sectionto the great mass
of Compensation Law from otherstates,as found in legalpub-
licationsof a generalnature, includingCyc, Corpus Juris,
L.R. A.,A. L.R.,RulingCase Law ,and especiallyinNegligence
and Compensation Cases Annotated . The cases in American
Digest (West Pub .Co.),are linkedto the varioussectionsof the
book by referencesto the Key Number System inthatDigest.
The Kentucky investigatorswill,by thisfeature,be given direct
referenceto the same subjectas treatedby the opinionsof the
courts of other states,and willbe given accessto the entirebody
of the law on the subjectin Kentucky as wellas in allother
states of the Union .
Parallelreferencesshow notonlythepagesoftheKentucky
Reports and Southwestern Reporter on which the cases are
published,but where Kentucky caseshave been reportedinthe
Lawyers Reports Annotated , American Law Reports, Ruling
Case Law ,and Negligenceand CompensationCases,references
willbe made to the case as publishedin thoseworks . The notes
to the Kentucky cases in those publicationsare of very high
value .

WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
Full textof the Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act is
given,with allamendments to date,and with referencesto both
the Kentucky Acts,and the Kentucky Statutes. This permits
a tracingof the historyof each sectionwith allamendments .
The forms preparedby the Board willbe included, with
directionsfortheiruse and explanatorynotes. Other forms
necessaryin practicingcasesunder the Compensation Law are
included,suchas petitionforreview,answer,orders,judgments,
stipulations, transcriptof evidence, etc.
No attempt has been made to writea textbook on Compensa-
tion Law . The purpose of the authors has been to compile a
thoroughlyannotatedCompensation Act, featuringthe points
decidedby theBoard and by theCourts,ratherthanany personal
opinion of the authors.
The indexhas been preparedwith great care and preci-
sion. Itrefersnot onlyto the varioussectionsof the Act,but
givesfullreferencesto the annotations. It is prepared from
both an analyticaland a practicalstandpoint,and includesthe
"word and phrase" or common sensesystemof indexing.
In conclusion I desire to state that this new edition is
almostentirelythe work ofmy colleague,FredP. Caldwell,and
I am surethat the bar of the State willbe as gratefulto him as I
am forhispainstakingeffortsto bringmy oldwork up to date.
LOUISVILLE, KY .,July5,1927.
NICHOLAS H. DOSKER .

entries.ocr_text:
PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION
The passageof a Workmen's Compensation Law by the
1916 General Assembly marks a new epoch in the development
of the law of thisState. It puts Kentucky in the frontrank of
progressiveAmerican Commonwealths . This law willundoubt-
edly prove to be a most beneficialand praiseworthypieceof
legislation. The cumbersome and often unfairremedies of the
common law are displacedby a system of rulesand remedies
which aim to do substantialjusticeto allconcerned,fairlyand
impartially. Naturally,theremovalofa largebody oflaw,and
the substitutionforitof new rulesand new procedure, means a
considerableupheaval of the establishedorder of things. While
the decisionsof our Court ofAppeals willmany times be valuable
in construingcertainportionsof the new law,itwill, untilthe
Court of Appeals has settleditsmeaning , be necessaryto go to
the decisionsof other compensation statesfor precedents.
The purpose of thisbook isto gather togethersome of the
more important authoritiesapplicableto the Kentucky law under
the sectionsof thisAct on the constructionof which they may
throw some light. A reasonableexplanationof the meaning of
the Act has alsobeen attempted . Ifthisbook isof assistanceto
the bar of thisState in facilitatingthe searchforauthoritiesand
ingivinga fairknowledgeofthe generalprinciplesinvolved,it
willhave serveditspurpose. Itisnot withinthe scopeofthis
work nor isitpossiblein a volume of thissize,to give exhaustive
treatmentto everyquestionraisedby thisAct.
The author acknowledges the valuable assistanceafforded
by the extensiveand thorough treatmentof thissubjectby
Walter M. Glass in Lawyers' Reports Annotated,1916 A and
alsoby Negligenceand Compensation Cases Annotated.
LOUISVILLE , KY .,July 15, 1916.
NICHOLAS H. DOSKER .

INTRODUCTION
TO FIRST EDITION
Von Jhering in The Strugglefor Law says: "All of the great
achievements which the history of the law has to record- the
abolitionof slavery,of serfdom,the freedom of landed property,
of industry, of conscience- have had to be won by the most
violentstruggles, which often lastedfor centuries. Not infre-
quentlystreamsofbloodand everywhererightstrampledunder
foot mark the way which law has traveledduring such conflict.
For the law is a Saturn devouring his own children. The law
can renew itsyouth only by breaking with itsown past."
Great and sudden changes in the body of law have usually
been ushered in by great national or industrialcataclysms.
In the briefspace of sixyears, an enormous branch of the law
has been uprootedbodilyintwo-thirdsoftheUnitedStatesand
supplanted by an entirelynew system of rules and remedies.
I referto the substitutionof Workmen's Compensation Acts
forthe Law of Negligence,as touchingthe relationof Master
and Servant. So tremendous isthe impetus thismovement has
gathered that it is not a far stretchof the imagination to say
that the next decade will find the law of Master and Servant a
memory . Great as has been this change , the accomplishment
of ithas been effectedwithout a great struggle.
Negligence, as developed under the common law , was
regarded in the nature of a crime. It was a personal matter
between the Master and the Servant. The one sought to punish
the other by suing for damages. For thisreason, untilthe
passageof the Lord Campbell'sAct, an actionfornegligence
died with the person. There were certaindegrees of negligence
and for certainflagrantkinds, punitiveas well as compensatory
damages were allowed. There isstillin every actionfordamages
thisquasi criminalattributeof negligence. The great common
law defenses- the doctrinesof contributorynegligence, fellow
servant, and assumed risk, were gradually developed. They ,

LAW OF KENTUCKY
accidents are the result of the natural hazard of the business .
It certainlyisnot fairforthe employe to bear the financial
lossresultingfrom the natural hazard of the businessin which
he isengaged ; neitherisitfairfor the employer to assume this
lossentirely. Workmen's Compensation Acts are based upon
thetheorythattheextracostdue totheiroperationisultimately
borne by societyat large. It was well said in an editorialin
The Outlookof March 1, 1913 : “When a machine isinjured in
the course of itsuse,the owner of the machine bears the costof
theinjuryand chargesittotheexpenseofproduction,forwhich
he receivespayment as he sellshisgoods. When ,however, a
workman isinjuredinthecourseofhisemployment,thecostof
theinjurycomes upon him,who can illaffordto bearit; and if
hisinjuryisserious,resultinginlongincapacityforwork,or in
death,hisfamilyisdraftedintothatgreatarmy of dependents
that isa reproach to our civilization. There is no reason that
common sense can accept why the costin human efficiencyand
human lifeof the production of the things that people need
should not be charged to the account ofthatproduction,justas is
chargedthecostofinjurytomachinery."
That inequalitiesamong those injuredexistedunder the old
system ispatentto even a casualobserver. Some men badly
injuredwithoutany faulton theirparthave receivednothing
and others with practicallyno injurieshave , through fraud or
perjury,receivedmany timeswhat theywereentitledto. Under
the contingentsystem offeesin personalinjury cases, attorneys
have almost universallyreceivedfar more than theirjust share
of the amount recovered by persons injured. There can be no
questionof the factthat the old system was bad . This was long
ago recognizedabroad,but ithas onlybeen inthelastfew years
that our publicconsciencehas been aroused on thissubject. The
successfulexperimentsofEurope withWorkmen's Compensation
Laws led to an attempt to relievethe situationhere by similar
laws. The resulthas been that Compensation Laws have been
passed in thirty-two statesand territories,comprising over eighty
per cent of the totalpopulationof the United States, and in

entries.ocr_text:
WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
The Workmen's Compensationideawas firsttriedout in
Germany . In 1884itwas ingeneraloperationthere. The plan
has been enlargedand developed untilnow they have sickness,
accidentand disabilityinsuranceallofwhich ispaidforby the
employersand the employes, who arecompelledto contribute
toa fund whichismanaged by officerschosenfromtheemploying
and working classes. The whole system isunder government
supervision.
In England various attempts were made to introduce this
system, but itwas not until1897 that a law was passed and be-
came effective. There the plan is differentfrom what it is in
Germany and the benefitsof the law are administeredby the
employersdirectorthroughinsurancecompanieswho guarantee
their risk under the law .
In thiscountry variousstateshave adopted differentplans.
In almost every instancethe law isadministeredby Industrial
Boards. The benefitsgrantedby the Acts are very similar,
differingchieflyinthepercentofaverageweeklywages paidto
injuredworkmen ; but the methods by which the payment of
thesebenefitsare guaranteed, fallintothreedifferentgroups:
First,where thereisa monopoly by stockinsurancecompanies;
second, where thereis a monopoly ofStatefunds- commonly
termed State Insurance where the State financesthe operation
of theAct out ofitstreasury, and third,where thereiscompeti-
tionbetweenStatefunds,stockcompanies,mutuals,reciprocals,
inter-insurers or some combination of these insurance carriers .
Kentucky's1914 Act- which founderedon the rocks of
unconstitutionality- belonged to the third group. It was
hastilyand unscientificallydrawn, and was passed in a like
manner . The State was fortunate in the fact that this law
was not upheldbecausethe experiencethus gainedpaved the
way fora scientificallydrawn law. In thetestcaseofKentucky
StateJournalCo. v .Workmen's CompensationBoard,161 Ky .
562, 170 S. W. 1166, and in the modificationand extensionof
that opinion, 162 Ky .387, 172 S. W. 674, the Court of Appeals
showed how an Act could be drawn that would not conflictwith
the constitutionalprovisions,sections54 and 241.

fieldof the specialistsin personalinjurycaseswillremain un-

classification.title: AN ACT To provide an elective system of workmen's compensation for industrial accidents
classification.summary: This act establishes an elective system for workmen's compensation for industrial accidents in Kentucky. It outlines the process for employers and employees to elect coverage, defines the rights and liabilities of all parties involved, provides for medical and surgical care, sets compensation rates for injuries or death, establishes methods for insurance and securing payments, makes minors sui juris for certain purposes, creates a board to administer the act with specified duties and powers, imposes taxes on insurance premiums and self-insured employers for the board's maintenance, and provides for appeals to the courts. It also repeals conflicting laws.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the legislature. The category is 'labor' as it directly relates to employment and workplace conditions.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Application and Coverage
classification.summary: This statute outlines the applicability of the Workmen's Compensation Act in Kentucky for the year 1927. It applies to employers with three or more employees in the same occupation, excluding domestic employment, agriculture, steam railways, and common carriers covered by U.S. law. It specifically includes operators of threshing machines. The act covers personal injuries and death sustained by employees arising out of and in the course of employment, with exceptions for pre-existing diseases unless directly resulting from a traumatic injury. It explicitly includes injuries or death from inhaling noxious gases or smoke ('bad air') in mines, and any gas inhalation. Employers and employees not covered by the act can voluntarily opt-in through a written application to the Board.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns workmen's compensation and its scope of application. It details which employers and employees are covered by the act, the types of injuries it addresses, and specific exclusions and inclusions. There is no mention of race, and the provisions do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the references to 'Acts 1916, Chap.33', 'Acts 1918, Chap.176', etc.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statute discusses the purpose of the Workmen's Compensation Act. It highlights the Act's aim to provide a system for employers and employees to avoid personal injury litigation, ensuring prompt and reasonable compensation for accidental injuries sustained in employment under fixed rules and without friction. The Act established a Board to summarily determine factual questions, offer relief to claimants, settle claims through arbitration-like methods, and relieve courts of factual determinations. The overarching goal is a speedy and inexpensive settlement for injuries, a scientific adjustment of compensation for industrial injuries, and the replacement of old common law remedies with new machinery for processing claims and making compensation awards.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Purposes
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the legislative intent behind the law. Its primary purposes include relieving courts of factual determinations in compensation cases, providing compensation to dependents of deceased employees, and ensuring injured employees receive benefits regardless of negligence. The act is founded on the idea that workplace injuries and loss of manpower should be considered a cost of production, borne by the industry and ultimately the consumer. It also aims to address long legal delays and technicalities faced by injured workers.

classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act from 1927 discusses the interpretation and construction of the Act. It emphasizes that the Act should be liberally construed to achieve its purpose of providing a speedy and scientific adjustment of compensation for employee injuries. It also notes that the Act differs from others in its requirement for a traumatic injury by accident for disease compensation.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the legal interpretation of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It focuses on how courts should construe the statute, emphasizing a liberal interpretation to benefit injured employees. There is no mention of race, racial discrimination, or any provisions that would suggest it is a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is 'labor' as it directly pertains to worker compensation. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled statute book referencing a specific act.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act Construction
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines principles for its interpretation, emphasizing liberal construction to achieve its purpose. It clarifies that the Act provides for separate actions by injured employees and dependents of fatally injured employees, and that common law negligence rules do not apply. Claims are considered ex contractu, governed by the lex loci contractus.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Workmen's Compensation Act defines the criteria for establishing the relationship of master and servant, focusing on the employer's control over the work and the right to hire and fire. It cites previous court cases and clarifies that a general employer is not liable for the negligent injuries of a servant loaned to another, unless the general employer retains control. It also notes a specific case where the master-servant relationship was held not to exist.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Employer Liability
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statute discusses employer liability under the Workmen's Compensation Act, particularly in cases of business sales, changes in management, and the definition of the master-servant relationship. It clarifies when an employer remains liable even after selling a business if the employee is unaware of the sale, and defines a master as one who has the right to direct what shall be done, who shall do it, and how it shall be done. It also touches upon situations where the master-servant relationship continues even when the employee is not actively working.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Exemptions
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act outlines exemptions from the Act. It specifies that employers with fewer than three employees in the same occupation, employers of domestic help, and employers engaged in agriculture (with an exception for threshing machine operators) are generally exempt. It also clarifies the application of the Act to highway contractors, coal companies, and receivers, and discusses who is considered an employee.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Definition of Employee
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act defines who qualifies as an 'employee' for the purposes of the act. It specifies that at least three employees must be regularly engaged in the same occupation or business for an employer to be covered. It also clarifies that payment by job, day, or piece, as well as being a student workman or an officer/director, does not preclude one from being an employee. The definition is to be interpreted in its ordinary sense and according to the law of master and servant.

It cites several court cases to establish these legal definitions and also addresses how an employer recognizes the employer-employee relationship through compensation registration and the hiring of assistants by workmen.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 clarifies eligibility for compensation in various employment scenarios. It addresses cases involving peace officers in mining camps, employees working under assumed names, and those who misrepresented their age or marital status when securing employment. The core principle is that if an injury arises out of and in the course of employment, compensation is generally due, regardless of certain personal circumstances or the specific nature of the employee's role (e.g., peace officer).

classification.title: Workmen Comp-1927
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act discusses who qualifies as an employee for compensation purposes, specifically addressing stockholders, officers of corporations, and municipal employees. It clarifies that individuals with an interest in the business, such as officers or stockholders, can still be considered employees and eligible for compensation under certain circumstances. It also states that regularly employed municipal employees, excluding public officers, are included in the Act.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Definition of 'Domestic' Employee
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies the definition of a 'domestic' employee, distinguishing between those in a private home and those employed in commercial enterprises like hotels. It establishes that hotel workers, even those performing duties similar to domestic servants, are considered industrial employees covered by the Act, as their work is integral to a commercial business. The text cites several court cases to support this interpretation.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Employes - Agricultural Employment
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies which employees are covered. It specifies that employees in lodging houses are covered if injured while performing duties related to the cleanliness and good order of the establishment. It also clarifies that persons employed in manufacturing establishments or large hotels are considered industrial employees, not domestic servants. Crucially, it explicitly exempts farmers, agriculturists, and laborers employed in agriculture from the Act's provisions, with an amendment in 1924 adding an exception for operators of threshing machines.

It also explicitly exempts agricultural workers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of Kentucky - Maritime Jurisdiction
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 clarifies the application of the Act to maritime employment. It establishes that while admiralty jurisdiction is exclusive to federal courts, employers and employees engaged in maritime service can voluntarily accept the benefits of the Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act. If accepted, it becomes a binding contract that supersedes other liability measures. The state has jurisdiction over the Ohio River to the low water mark on the north or northwest side. However, a State Compensation Act is not applicable to a person injured while employed on a maritime contract, as per federal precedent.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Definition of Accident
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act defines what constitutes an "accident" for the purposes of the act. It clarifies that "personal injuries by accident" and "accidental injury" require an accident to arise out of and in the course of employment. Death is only compensated if it results from accidental injury. The term "accident" is used in its popular sense, meaning something unusual, unexpected, and undesigned, and an injury caused by negligence or carelessness is still considered an accident.

classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation law defines what constitutes an accident arising out of and in the course of employment. It clarifies that an accident occurs if the employee is reasonably performing their duties within their employed time and location, and the risk was foreseeable. The statute emphasizes that both 'arising out of' (origin/cause) and 'in the course of' (time, place, circumstances) are conjunctive requirements for compensation, and neither can be missing for recovery. It cites several court cases to support these definitions.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statute
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation statute from 1927 discusses conditions under which injuries or deaths are compensable. It clarifies that diseases are not compensable if not resulting from traumatic injury, and that accidents caused by forces of nature are not compensable if the risk is common to the public and not accentuated by employment. It cites several court cases to support these interpretations, including cases related to heat prostration and industrial accidents.

It cites several court cases from various states (Maine, Kansas) to illustrate these principles.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1927 Workmen's Compensation Act and discusses legal precedents for determining work-related injuries. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The focus is solely on labor law and compensation for injuries sustained during employment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Contact with Irritating Substances
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statutes addresses workmen's compensation claims related to disabilities caused by contact with irritating substances. It clarifies that compensation is not payable if the disability is a result of a series of applications over time rather than a single, identifiable incident, and distinguishes between gradual disease development and personal or traumatic injury by accident. It also references English compensation law for comparison.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation - Fainting Spell - Falling
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which an employee can receive compensation for an injury resulting from a fall due to fainting. It requires proof that the employment conditions caused the fainting and fall, or that the employee was exposed to a greater hazard from fainting and falling due to the employment than they would have been otherwise. The case cited, Lell v. William Kopp Co., illustrates that if the hazard is not specific to the employment, the injury does not arise out of the employment, even if it occurred during the course of employment.

classification.title: Workmen Comp-1927
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation law discusses when bites by poisonous insects, reptiles, and animals are considered industrial accidents. It establishes that such injuries are compensable if they arise out of and in the course of employment, particularly if the employment exposes the employee to a greater hazard of being bitten due to the nature of the work or subjects them to a special danger.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - General Illustrations of Accidents Arising Out of and in Course of Employment
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act from 1927 provides general illustrations of what constitutes an accident arising out of and in the course of employment. It clarifies that injuries sustained while performing acts for personal comfort, such as quenching thirst or relieving hunger, are compensable if reasonably necessary. It also addresses injuries resulting from errors in judgment due to perceived danger, even if the fear is unwarranted. The text emphasizes that compensation is due when the employment exposes the employee to a greater hazard or a special danger of injury.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Death Resulting from Accidental Injury
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the conditions under which death resulting from an accidental injury is compensable. It specifies that the death must be a direct result of an accidental injury arising out of and in the course of employment, occurring within two years of the accident. The statute also details the burden of proof on the plaintiff to establish the causal link between the accident and the death, and prohibits compensation based on speculation or probability not reasonably established by fact.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act Amendments
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky legal statutes discusses amendments to the Workmen's Compensation Act. It clarifies that the act covers injuries or death from inhaling noxious gases or smoke in mines, as amended in 1924. It also specifies that only diseases resulting from traumatic injury by accident are compensable, not occupational diseases or pre-existing conditions. Diseases not caused by traumatic injury but by employer negligence are actionable at law. An example is given of a disability caused by heart inflammation from bad air in a mine, which was not initially compensable under the Act but became so with the 1924 amendment.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Interpretation of Accidental Injury

It clarifies that pre-existing diseases or diseases not directly resulting from a traumatic injury are not compensable. Cases cited illustrate that heart failure due to pre-existing conditions like angina pectoris, even if exacerbated by exertion, is not considered a traumatic injury by accident. However, neurotic conditions directly resulting from a traumatic injury are compensable.

entries.ocr_text:
§ 3 OF ACT] LAW OF KENTUCKY § 64

**SEC. 81. MISCELLANEOUS CASES REFERRING TO SECTION 3 OF THE ACT.**

**SEC. 64. (SEC. 3 OF THE ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4882.)**

**WHEN THE ACT APPLIES.** Whereas, at the time of the injury, both employer and employe have elected to furnish or accept compensation under the provisions of this Act for a personal injury, received by an employe by accident and arising out of and in the course of his employment, or for death resulting from such injury, within two years thereafter, the employer shall be liable to provide and pay compensation under the provisions of this Act and shall be released from all other liability whatsoever; provided, however, that if injury or death result to an employe through the deliberate intention of his employer to produce such injury or death, the employe or his dependent, as herein defined, shall receive the amount provided in this Act in a lump sum to be used, if they so desire, to prosecute the employer, and said dependents shall be permitted to bring suit against said employer for any amount they may desire; that if injury or death result to an employe through the deliberate intention of his employer to produce such injury or death, the employe or his dependents as herein defined shall have the privilege to take under this Act, or in lieu thereof to have a cause of action at law against such employer as if this Act had not been passed, for such damages so sustained by the employe, his dependents or personal representatives as may be recoverable at law. If a suit is brought under this section, all right to compensation under the provisions of this Act shall thereby be waived and void as to all persons, and if a claim is made for the payment of compensation or any other benefit provided by this Act, all rights to sue the employer for damages on account of such injury or death shall thereby be waived and void as to all persons.

Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore or hereafter contained no employe or dependent of any employe shall be entitled to receive compensation on account of any injury to or death of an employe caused by a wilful self-inflicted injury, wilful misconduct or intoxication of such employe. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 3, p. 355.)

classification.title: Workmen Compensation Act - Application and Liability
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which an employer is liable to provide compensation to an employee for injuries or death sustained during employment. It specifies that if both employer and employee elect to be covered by the Act, the employer is liable for compensation and released from other liability. It also provides recourse for employees or dependents if the injury or death results from the employer's deliberate intention, allowing them to receive a lump sum for prosecution or pursue a lawsuit as if the Act had not passed. The statute also states that no compensation will be provided for injuries or death caused by the employee's wilful self-inflicted injury, wilful misconduct, or intoxication.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns workmen's compensation and the conditions for employer liability in cases of employee injury or death. It details the election process for compensation, employer's release from other liabilities, and provisions for deliberate intent by the employer. It also excludes compensation for employee's self-inflicted injuries, misconduct, or intoxication. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory provisions within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The category is 'labor' as it directly relates to employment and worker protections. It covers scenarios such as an employer's acceptance of the act, an employee's age and its impact on their binding to the act, and the admissibility of evidence to prove age. It also touches upon the employer's right to waive an employee's failure to accept the act's provisions and the procedure for death cases.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act Provisions
classification.summary: This text discusses provisions of the Workmen's Compensation Act in Kentucky, specifically concerning the rights of an employee's personal representative to recover damages for death caused by employer negligence. It clarifies that if an employee accepted the Act's provisions, their representative cannot maintain a separate action for damages, as the Act provides compensation in lieu of other liability. It also references Kentucky Constitution, section 241, regarding recovery for death by negligence, and section 54, concerning legislative power to limit recovery amounts.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 in Kentucky. It focuses on the legal implications for an employee's personal representative seeking damages for a death caused by employer negligence, particularly when the employee had accepted the provisions of the Act. The text details how the Act supersedes the right to sue for damages, offering compensation instead. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of 1916 - Negligence Does Not Defeat Recovery
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1916, as interpreted in 1927, clarifies that an employee's negligence does not automatically defeat their claim for compensation. Recovery is only barred if the injury is due to the employee's willful misconduct or intoxication. The act abrogates the common law rule where an employee's contributory negligence would prevent recovery, even if the employer was also negligent.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of Kentucky - Negligence Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of Kentucky clarifies that an employee's negligence, whether ordinary or gross, does not prevent them from receiving compensation for injuries sustained during the course of employment. The act aims to eliminate questions of negligence in compensation claims, except for specific circumstances outlined in Ky. Stats., sec. 4882. It also notes that injuries caused by an employee's gross carelessness or foolhardiness are still considered accidents arising out of and in the course of employment.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 clarifies the conditions under which death resulting from an accidental injury is compensable. It discusses the burden of proof on claimants, the concept of proximate cause and intervening acts, and situations where death may result indirectly from an injury (e.g., through surgery or blood poisoning). It also states that if a claim is dismissed by the Workmen's Compensation Board for lack of jurisdiction because the injury is not compensable under the statute, this dismissal does not prevent the employee from filing a lawsuit at law for negligence based on the same claim. The text also notes that an employee can rely on an employer obeying statutory provisions and does not assume risks caused by the employer's failure to do so.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It deals with the legal framework for compensating workers for injuries sustained on the job. The content focuses on the definition of compensable injuries, the chain of causation for death resulting from an injury, and the procedural aspects of filing claims with the Workmen's Compensation Board versus pursuing legal action for negligence. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it appears to be part of a compiled body of statutes, as indicated by the section numbering and references to other sections within the act.

The amendment from 1920 allowed the Board to extend treatment periods or expense limits.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Sections 84-85
classification.summary: These sections of the Workmen's Compensation Act address the employer's responsibility to provide medical services to injured employees and the employee's right to select their own physician. Section 84 explains the rationale behind employer-provided medical care, emphasizing the employer's ability to judge physician efficiency and their interest in minimizing injury results and securing early recovery. Section 85 clarifies that an employee can still receive compensation benefits even if they choose and pay for their own physician, provided the physician is state-licensed and the employee was unaware of any improper treatment. However, if the employer is already providing a competent physician, they are not liable for services from a different physician chosen by the employee without the employer's notice.

classification.title: Workmen Comp-1927.pdf, page 276, year 1927
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statute outlines the provisions for "First Aid" in workers' compensation cases. It clarifies the meaning of "first aid" in the context of employer liability insurance and workers' compensation. It details the rights of both the employee and employer regarding the selection of physicians for initial emergency treatment and subsequent care. The employee can call a physician in an emergency at the employer's expense, and the employer can retain or substitute physicians. If the employer is aware of the disability and does not offer to substitute their physician, they are liable for the employee's chosen physician's services within the Act's limits. If the employer neglects to furnish medical aid, the employee can procure a physician at the employer's expense. The section also clarifies that emergency first aid provisions are not inconsistent with hernia operation requirements.

classification.title: HERNIA AS AN ACCIDENT
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for claiming compensation for a hernia as an accident under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It specifies that there must be a definite injury resulting in the hernia, the hernia must have appeared suddenly and immediately after the injury, and the hernia must not have existed prior to the injury. If compensation is liable, the employer must provide surgical treatment, with benefits potentially increased to $200 if an operation is performed. If the employee refuses the operation, the employer has the right to a medical examination.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns workmen's compensation for hernias. It details the conditions under which a hernia can be considered an accident for compensation purposes and outlines the employer's responsibilities regarding surgical treatment. § 4a OF ACT] LAW OF KENTUCKY § 88

in section forty-nine hundred and eighteen hereof. If it be shown by such examination that the employe has any chronic disease or is otherwise in such physical condition so as to render it more than ordinarily unsafe to submit to such operation, he shall, if unwilling to submit to the operation, be entitled to compensation for disability under the general provisions of this Act. If the examination does not disclose the existence of disease or other physical condition rendering the operation more than ordinarily unsafe, and the employe, with the knowledge of the result of such examination, thereafter refuses to submit to such operation, he shall be entitled to compensation for disability under the general provisions of this Act for not exceeding one year. If the employe submits to the operation he shall, in addition to the surgical benefits herein provided for, be entitled to compensation for HIS ACTUAL DISABILITY FOLLOWING SUCH OPERATION. If the hernia result in death within one year after it is sustained, or the operation result in death, such death shall be deemed a result of the injury causing such hernia and compensated accordingly under the provisions of this Act. This paragraph shall not apply where the employe has refused to submit to an operation which has been found by the examination herein provided for not to be more than ordinarily unsafe. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 4a, p. 357, as amended Acts 1924, Chap. 70, p. 171.)

Section 4a was amended by Acts 1924, Chap. 70, p. 171, 173; so as to make the employe who submits to the operation entitled to compensation for his actual disability following such operation, instead of "compensation for twenty-six weeks," as the original Act provided.

(a) The general rule is that where a hernia results from a strain or an overexertion of some kind while the workman is performing the duties of his employment it is a personal injury by accident within the meaning of Workmen's Compensation Acts.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Hernia Notice Requirements
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the notice requirements for employees reporting hernias sustained in the workplace. It specifies that employees must notify their employer as soon as practicable after the injury. In cases where there is no immediate emergency, the employer is entitled to notice and an opportunity to provide a physician. The statute emphasizes a stricter adherence to notice requirements for hernia cases compared to other injuries, particularly because a prerequisite for compensation is that the hernia must have appeared suddenly and immediately following the injury.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Medical Aid Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies provisions related to an injured employee's refusal or failure to accept medical aid. It states that an employee is not required to follow the medical advice or treatment furnished by the defendant employer. If an employee seeks competent treatment elsewhere, it will not be considered an unreasonable failure to accept medical aid, provided the outcome is not materially different. However, if an employee chooses their own physician, they must bear the expense. The section also notes that if nature achieves the same result as a proposed operation, the employee's disability is not aggravated by refusal of treatment.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Waiting Period and Payment of Compensation
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act establishes a seven-day waiting period before compensation is payable for disability. Compensation is to be paid on the employer's regular pay days, commencing with the first pay day after seven days from the injury. Interest at 6% per annum is applied to past-due installments. The amendment reducing the waiting period from two weeks to seven days became effective on August 1, 1918.

It states that employers hiring employees within Kentucky to work partly or wholly outside the state may agree in writing to exempt injuries sustained outside the state from the act's coverage. In the absence of such an agreement, the act's remedies remain exclusive for injuries received outside the state.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act Interpretation
classification.summary: This section of the statute discusses the interpretation of workmen's compensation contracts, emphasizing that the provisions of the Compensation Act are to be considered as written into the contract. It cites several court cases from New Jersey and Colorado to support the principle that the place of the accident is irrelevant to the enforcement of a contract made under a specific state's statute, and that the lex loci contractus (law of the place where the contract was made) governs the construction of the contract and its legal obligations. It also notes that the statute implies a contract for compensation for injuries arising out of and in the course of employment, and that all other methods of compensation are relinquished under this implied contract.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the legal interpretation of workmen's compensation contracts and the application of statutory provisions to these contracts. It cites case law to establish that the place of the contract, not the place of the accident, determines the governing law. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices based on race within this text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Injuries Caused by Third Persons
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines the rights and remedies of an injured employee when the injury is caused by a third party, not the employer. The employee can choose to claim compensation from the employer, sue the third party for damages, or pursue both. However, the employee cannot collect compensation from both sources. If compensation is awarded, the employer or their insurance carrier, having paid or become liable for the compensation, gains the right to recover from the liable third party.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Third Party Claims
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 clarifies the rights and elections of claimants when an injury is caused by a third party. It states that settling a claim with a third party is competent evidence to show the claimant elected to proceed at common law for damages rather than filing a claim for compensation. Such a settlement acts as a valid plea in bar to an action for compensation. The statute also outlines the employer's subrogation rights against a third party if compensation is awarded, but notes that a prior settlement by the claimant can prevent the employer from exercising these rights.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to workmen's compensation claims involving third parties. It outlines the claimant's right to elect between seeking compensation from the employer or damages from a third party, and the legal implications of settling with a third party. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to a section of an Act and cites legal decisions, suggesting it's part of a compiled legal code or statute book.

§ 121 WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION [§ 10 OF ACT

any employe injured while in the employ of any one of his intermediate or subcontractors and engaged upon the subject-matter of the contract, to the same extent as the immediate employer. Any principal, intermediate or subcontractor (OR THEIR INSURANCE CARRIER), who shall pay compensation under the foregoing provision may recover the amount paid from any subordinate contractor through whom he may have been rendered liable under this section. Every claim to compensation under this section shall in the first instance be presented to and instituted against the immediate employer, but such proceeding shall not constitute a waiver of the employe's rights to recover compensation under this Act from the principal or intermediate contractor; provided, that the collection of full compensation from one employer shall bar recovery by the employe against any others; nor shall he collect from all a total compensation in excess of the amount for which his immediate employer is liable. This section shall apply only in cases where the injury occurred on, in or about the premises on which the principal contractor has undertaken to execute work or which are under his control otherwise or management. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 10, p. 360, as amended Acts 1922, Chap. 50, p. 167, but see note following.)

Section 10 of the Act was amended by Acts 1922, Chap. 50, pp. 167, 169, by inserting in the first paragraph thereof immediately after the words "or subcontractor" the words "or their insurance carrier."

Section 10 was amended by Acts 1924, Chap. 70, p. 174, so as to take the comma out of the first line after the word "principal," but the section as there amended omitted the words "or their insurance carrier" which had been inserted by the Act of 1922.

SEC 121. WHO ARE INDEPENDENT CONTRACTORS.

(a) Independent contractors are not covered by the Workmen's Compensation Act, and they cannot claim its benefits against their principals. Diamond Block Coal Co. v. Sparks, 209 Ky. 73, 272 S. W. 31.

The question to be determined is whether a person is an employe or an independent contractor. This question is

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Principal and Subcontractor Liability
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the liability of principal, intermediate, and subcontractors for employee injuries. It specifies that an employee injured while working for a subcontractor can claim compensation from the principal employer to the same extent as from their immediate employer. The principal employer who pays compensation can recover the amount from the subordinate contractor. Claims are initially filed against the immediate employer, but this does not waive the employee's right to claim from principal or intermediate contractors. However, collecting full compensation from one employer bars recovery from others, and the total compensation cannot exceed the amount for which the immediate employer is liable. This section applies only to injuries occurring on or about the premises under the principal contractor's control. It also clarifies that independent contractors are not covered by the Act.

It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. The focus is on the contractual relationships between employers and employees in the context of workplace injuries. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses labor law without any racial implications. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines the relationship between principal contractors and subcontractors regarding employee compensation. It establishes that employees can seek compensation from contractors above their immediate employer, but must first exhaust remedies against their immediate employer or their insurer. The principal contractor is responsible for ensuring all subcontractors have insured their liability under the Act. The section also clarifies that claims can only be made if the injury occurred on the principal contractor's premises or premises under their control. Steam railways are exempted from the Act in relation to their employees but may still be bound by this section.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Principal and Contractor Liability

It specifies that a principal is not liable for injuries to employees of an independent contractor if the work is not performed on the principal's premises or under their control. Case law examples are provided to illustrate these principles.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Minor Employment Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act, as amended in 1924, addresses the employment of minors. It states that a minor over sixteen years of age is considered sui juris (having full legal capacity) for the purposes of the Act, provided a parent, guardian, or legal authority certifies this fact. This certification prevents others from claiming compensation for injuries or death of the minor due to their minority. Payment of lump sum compensation to a minor is to be made to their guardian. The amendment also removed a clause that excluded cases of willful violation of laws regulating minor employment.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the legal age and capacity of minors for employment under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It outlines procedures for certifying a minor's age and designates who can receive compensation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the references to 'Acts 1916, Chap. 33' and 'Acts 1924, Chap. 70', which indicate specific legislative sessions and acts.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Minor Employes
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies the legal status of minor employees. It defines 'sui juris' as a person capable of contracting and binding themselves legally. The Act declares minors 'sui juris' for the purpose of making binding contracts to accept compensation, but not for all purposes. If compensation is paid in a lump sum, it must go to the minor's legal guardian, though they can receive weekly payments directly. Crucially, Section 11 of the Act states that no other person, including parents, has a cause of action or right to compensation for an injury to or death of a minor employee, as the minor is considered 'sui juris' for the purposes of the Act and should make the claim themselves.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal capacity of minor employees to enter into contracts for workmen's compensation and to claim compensation for injuries. It defines 'sui juris' and clarifies that minors are considered 'sui juris' for the purposes of the Workmen's Compensation Act, meaning they can make binding contracts for compensation and claim it themselves, though lump sum payments go to a guardian. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory provisions based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the statute's focus is solely on the legal status of minors in the context of labor law, with no racial implications.

WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION [§§ 12-15 OF ACT

SEC. 164. EFFECT ON DEPENDENTS OF IMPRISONMENT OF WORKMAN.

SEC. 128. (SEC. 12 OF THE ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4893.)
**TO WHOM COMPENSATION IS PAYABLE IN CASE OF DEATH.** If death results within two years from an accident for which compensation is payable under this Act, the employer or his insurer shall pay to the persons entitled to compensation, or, if none, then to the personal representative of the deceased employe reasonable burial expenses of a person of the standard of living of the deceased, not to exceed the sum of $75, and shall also pay to or for the following persons compensation as follows, to-wit:

(1) If there are no dependents, as herein defined, there shall be paid, in addition to the burial expenses and medical expenses, if any, otherwise provided for herein, the further sum of $100, payment to be made to the personal representative of the deceased employe.

(2) If there are one or more wholly dependent persons 65 per cent of the average weekly earnings of the deceased employe, but not to exceed $12 nor less than $5 per week shall be payable, all such payments to be made for the period between the date of death and 335 weeks after the date of accident to the employe, or until the intervening termination of dependency, but in no case to exceed the maximum sum of $4,000.

(3) If there are partly dependent persons, the payments shall be such part of what would be payable for total dependency as the partial dependency existing at the time of the accident to the employe may be proportionate to total dependency, all such payments to be made for the period between the date of death and 335 weeks after the date of the accident to the deceased employe, or until the intervening termination of dependency, but in no case to exceed in the aggregate of compensation on account of such death the maximum sum of $4,000.

Partial dependency shall be determined by the proportion of the earnings of the employe which have been contributed to such partial dependent during one year next preceding the date of injury; if the relation of partial dependency shall not have existed for one

The terms "brother" and "sister" include *step-brothers, step-sisters* and *brothers and sisters of the half blood or by adoption*, but *excludes married brothers or sisters unless actually dependent*. The term "grandchild" includes *children of adopted children and children of step-children*, but *excludes step-children of children or of adopted children and married children*. The term "parent" includes *step-parents and parents by adoption*. The words "adopted" and "adoption" as herein used include cases where the persons are legally adopted. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 14, p. 364.)

**SEC. 131. (SEC. 15 OF ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4896.) PAYMENT OF DEATH BENEFITS—CREDITS.** *Payment of death benefits, in good faith, to a supposed dependent or to a dependent subsequent in right to another or other dependents shall protect and discharge the employer and insurer unless and until the lawful dependent or dependents prior in right shall have given the employer or insurer written notice of his or their claim.* In case the employer or insurer is in doubt as to who are dependents or as to their respective rights, the *Board* shall, on application, decide and direct to whom payment shall be made, and payment made under such direction shall release the employer and insurer from all liability; *provided, however, that if an appeal be taken from the order of the Board directing payment, persons receiving payment under such order shall be required to furnish bond for the protection of adverse claimants pending the outcome of the proceedings.*

*In case death occurs as a result of the injury, after a period of total or partial disability, the period of disability shall be deducted from the total period of compensation and the benefits paid thereunder from the maximum allowed for the death respectively stated in section 12 hereof.* (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 15, p. 364.)

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statute - Partial Dependency Calculation
classification.summary: This statute outlines the method for calculating partial dependency in workmen's compensation cases. It specifies that the extent of partial dependency is determined by the proportion of the employee's earnings contributed to the dependent in the year preceding the injury. The calculation should use net contributions, meaning gross contributions minus the employee's own living expenses (board, clothing, incidentals). If direct records are unavailable, dependency can be estimated by subtracting the employee's estimated board and room at home from their total earnings.

Partially dependent persons can participate in awards alongside totally dependent individuals.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Abandonment by Wife
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies the definition of 'voluntary abandonment' by a wife in the context of dependency claims. It states that adulterous conduct by a wife, followed by her not living with or being supported by her husband, constitutes voluntary abandonment. The presumption of dependency for a wife is rebuttable if she has voluntarily abandoned her husband. The text also references divorce law to illustrate how abandonment can be established even if the wife is the one who physically leaves, provided the husband's conduct creates conditions preventing her from living with him peacefully. Applying this to a wife who in good faith attempts to divorce and remarries, it is considered voluntary abandonment by her, forfeiting her claim.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Law - Dependents
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Law addresses who qualifies as a dependent. It states that a common-law marriage recognized in another state will be recognized in Kentucky if the parties move there. However, it clarifies that the victim of a bigamous marriage, even if supported by the deceased, is not a dependent. It also notes that if a legal marriage did not exist, and common-law marriages are not recognized (as per Revised Statutes), a claimant cannot be a dependent. Furthermore, relationships violating Kentucky law do not qualify for dependency, and bigamous marriages do not establish dependency.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Dependents - Parents
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act clarifies the dependency status of parents for benefits. It states that parents are not conclusively presumed to be dependent but must have their dependency determined by the facts of each case at the time of the accident. Partial dependency is sufficient, and the ability of parents to earn wages or a livelihood does not automatically negate dependency. The key test is whether the contributions from the deceased employee were used for the family's support and were necessary, considering the family's station in life and the claimant's earnings. If parental support offsets the employee's contributions, then no dependency exists.

It specifies that there is no presumption of dependency for a child not living with or supported by the alleged parent at the time of the accident. Specifically, a child under sixteen is not entitled to compensation unless living in the household or actually supported by the deceased employee. It also addresses situations where a wife has abandoned her husband and retained custody of children, stating that neither the wife nor the children can recover compensation if the father was not supporting them at the time of his death. The statute clarifies that legitimacy is not the test of dependency and that for children over sixteen not incapacitated from wage earning, dependency is a question of fact.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Payments to Supposed Dependents
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act addresses how employers should handle payments to dependents when there are multiple claimants. It states that if an employer pays compensation in good faith to a dependent they believe is entitled to it, this payment will discharge their liability up to the point they receive written notice from another claimant. The employer can try to determine the rightful claimant, but if they pay incorrectly after receiving notice, they will not be discharged from liability. Applying to the Board for a decision will halt compensation until a ruling is made, and payment according to the Board's decision will relieve the employer of all other liability. Claimants can also file an application with the Board to establish their prior right as a dependent.

classification.title: TOTAL DISABILITY.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions and compensation for total disability under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It specifies that if an injury causes total disability for work, the employer shall pay the injured employee a weekly compensation equal to sixty-five percent of their average weekly earnings, with a maximum of fifteen dollars and a minimum of five dollars, excluding the first seven days of disability. It also references a court case where a portion of compensation was paid for the support of children during incarceration.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statute - Interpretation of Total Disability
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act, specifically § 16, interprets the meaning of total disability for injured employees. It clarifies that total disability occurs when an employee suffers a permanent loss of sight in both eyes that incapacitates them from working in an income-yielding occupation requiring eyesight. It further defines total disability as a condition resulting from injury that incapacitates the employee to the extent that their income-earning capacity is restricted to work that a totally blind person could normally perform, or if they lack arms, they can still utilize their eyes, feet, or mental faculties. The text also references case law regarding compensation for hip injuries and the inability to obtain work due to injury.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the interpretation of total disability under Kentucky's Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It focuses on the physical and functional limitations of an injured employee and their ability to earn income. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: TOTAL PERMANENT DISABILITY
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the conditions under which a disability is deemed total and permanent. It specifies that Section 16 of the Act enumerates six types of injuries that automatically qualify for total and permanent disability status. To establish a claim, the relationship of master and servant must be proven, and the injury must have resulted from an accident arising out of and in the course of employment. For injuries not listed in Section 16, the claimant bears the burden of proof.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statute - Disability Calculation
classification.summary: This statute, specifically referencing sections 4897 and 4901 of the Kentucky Statutes, clarifies how compensation for total disability is calculated when an employee has a previous injury. It states that a previous injury should be considered in the allowance for total disability resulting from a subsequent injury, aiming to prevent an employee from receiving more compensation than someone not previously maimed. The intent is to provide for permanently disabled employees without them becoming a public charge, while also accounting for pre-existing conditions.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Partial Permanent Disability
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Statutes, specifically Section 178, outlines the compensation for partial permanent disability due to specific injuries enumerated in a schedule. It states that for the loss of a thumb or a first finger, an employee shall receive 65% of their average weekly wages for a specified number of weeks (60 for a thumb, 45 for a first finger), as a form of compensation in lieu of all other compensation. The weekly compensation is capped between $5.00 and $12.00.

entries.ocr_text:
§ 18 OF ACT] LAW OF KENTUCKY § 178

*For the loss of a second finger, 65 per cent of the average (weekly) wages during 30 weeks.*

*For the loss of a third finger, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 20 weeks.*

*For the loss of a fourth finger, commonly known as the little finger, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 15 weeks.*

*The loss of the second, or distal phalange, of the thumb shall be considered to be equal to the loss of one-half of such thumb; the loss of more than one-half of such thumb shall be considered to be equal to the loss of the whole thumb.*

*The loss of the third, or distal phalange, of any finger, shall be considered to be equal to the loss of one-third of such finger.*

*The loss of the middle, or second phalange, of any finger shall be considered to be equal to the loss of two-thirds of such finger.*

*The loss of more than the middle and distal phalanges of any finger shall be considered to be equal to the loss of the whole finger; provided, however, that in no case will the amount received for more than one finger exceed the amount provided in this schedule for the loss of a hand.*

*For a loss of a metacarpal bone (bone of palm) for the corresponding thumb, finger or fingers above, add 10 weeks to the number of weeks as above.*

*For ankylosis (total stiffness of) or contractures (due to sears or injuries) which makes the fingers more than useless, the same number of weeks apply to such finger or fingers (not thumb) as given above.*

*For the loss of a hand 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 150 weeks.*

*For the loss of an arm 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 200 weeks.*

*For the loss of one of the toes, other than the great toe, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 10 weeks.*

*For the loss of the great toe, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 30 weeks.*

*The loss of more than two-thirds of any toe shall be considered equal to the loss of the whole toe.*

§ 179 WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION [§ 18 OF ACT

*The loss of less than two-thirds of any toe shall be considered to be equal to the loss of one-half toe.* For the loss of a foot, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 125 weeks.

*For the loss of a leg, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 200 weeks, or the total and permanent loss of the sight of an eye, 65 per cent of the average weekly wages during 100 weeks.*

*In all other cases of permanent partial disability, including any disfigurement which will impair the future usefulness or occupational opportunities of the injured employe, compensation shall be determined according to the percentage of disability, taking into account, among other things, any previous disability, the nature of the physical injury or disfigurement, the occupation of the injured employe and age at the time of injury; the compensation paid therefor shall be 65 per cent of the average weekly earnings of the employe, but not less than $5.00 nor more than $12.00, multiplied by the percentage of disability caused by the injury, for such period as the Board may determine, not exceeding 335 weeks nor a maximum sum of $4,000. Whenever the weekly payments under this paragraph would be less than $3.00 per week, the period may be shortened and the payments correspondingly increased to that amount. Where compensation, except as provided in sections 4 and 5 of this Act, is paid under any other provision of this Act the period during which such other compensation is paid and the amount thereof shall be deducted respectively from the maximum period and maximum amount which may be paid under this paragraph.* (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 18, p. 366.)

**SEC. 179. SCHEDULED AND NON-SCHEDULED INJURIES—GENERALLY.**

(a) Almost every one of the American Compensation Acts has a specific injury schedule, showing definitely certain injuries, which in themselves constitute permanent partial disability, and the schedule also prescribes the length of time during which the compensation benefits are to be paid on account of said injuries.

Section 18 of the Kentucky Act contains the schedule for specific injuries. After providing for various other specific injuries which are in lieu of all other claims for compensation,

classification.title: SEC. 183. LEG OR FOOT.
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Compensation Act, enacted in 1927, defines how compensation is determined for the loss of a leg or foot in industrial accidents. It specifies that the foot is the part of the leg below the ankle joint, and the loss of a leg is defined as losing so much of the limb as to deprive it of its function as a support or means of locomotion. It also outlines that compensation for an amputation between 7.5 and 8 inches above the ankle joint should be less than for a total leg loss but more than for a foot loss, based on the percentage of permanent disability.

classification.title: SEC. 186. "LOSS" OF MEMBER.
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky statutes, enacted in 1927, clarifies the definition of "loss" of a bodily member for the purposes of workers' compensation. It distinguishes between the physical severance of a member and impairment, stating that only actual physical loss is classified as a permanent partial disability under a specific schedule. It also notes that for the loss of an eyeball, the general provision applies, not the specific provision for loss of sight. The statute emphasizes that compensation for impairment not resulting in actual severance is based on the impairment of earning capacity, not solely on the percentage of impairment to the member.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses workers' compensation for the loss of bodily members. It defines 'loss' as actual physical severance and differentiates it from impairment. The text does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses a labor law issue without any racial undertones. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Interpretation of Injury and Disability
classification.summary: This statute interprets the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927, specifically concerning the compensation for injuries to a 'member' of the body. It clarifies that if a member is injured and impaired but not severed, the specific schedule for loss of a limb does not apply. Instead, such cases fall under the general provisions for permanent partial disability or total disability if the claimant is continuously unable to work. The text references previous court cases and annotations to support its interpretation.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statute - Permanent Partial Impairment
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky law details how permanent partial impairment for injured workmen should be compensated. It emphasizes that compensation should be based on earnings at the time of injury and consider the claimant's overall condition, future usefulness, and occupational opportunities, rather than just a difference in wages or speculative future jobs. It also states that even if an injured worker continues in the same job and earns the same wages, they are still entitled to compensation if their physical efficiency is impaired.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Disability and Death Benefits
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act addresses the compensability of injuries that aggravate pre-existing conditions or result in death. It emphasizes that the Act covers all employees, regardless of their physical condition, and that employers take employees as they find them. The statute clarifies that if an injury causes or accelerates death, or results in disability, compensation should be paid, even if the employee had a pre-existing condition that predisposed them to the outcome. Two case examples illustrate this principle: one involving a pre-existing heart condition aggravated by an injury, and another involving an employee with a club foot who suffered an amputation due to an accident.

It focuses on the employer-employee contractual relationship and the principle that employers accept employees with their existing physical states. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to sections within a book of statutes and cites previous decisions, suggesting it's part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the content is clearly focused on labor law and has no discernible racial component.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Combined Effect of Previous and Subsequent Injury
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act addresses how compensation is handled when an employee sustains a subsequent injury that exacerbates a previous one. It states that the employer responsible for the subsequent injury is liable only for the compensation due to the resulting condition, minus any compensation that would have been due for the prior injury if it had been compensated under the law. It also outlines the employer's right to offer suitable work to an injured employee.

It also addresses situations where an injury is the sole cause of death, making apportionment clauses inapplicable.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Review of Award or Order by the Board
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the process for reviewing awards or orders made by the Board. It allows the Board to review any award or order on its own motion or upon application from an interested party if there is a change in conditions, mistake, or fraud. The Board can then end, diminish, or increase the compensation, or change or revoke its previous order. Such reviews require notice to the parties involved and do not affect sums already paid under a previous order.

classification.title: REVIEW OF AWARD - GENERALLY
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the conditions under which an award or order can be reviewed. A review requires due notice to all interested parties and must be based on a showing of changed conditions, a mistake in the original award, or fraud by either party. The process is akin to applying for a new trial and is governed by specific statutory provisions, particularly section 21 of the Compensation Act (Ky. Stats., sec. 4902). The Compensation Board has the authority to reopen cases for review based on these grounds, even after compensation has been agreed upon and released, and is not limited to only diminishing or increasing awards. Approved agreed settlements become enforceable awards and can be set aside or reviewed under the same statutory provisions. Section 4902 of the Kentucky Statutes grants the Board broad power to reopen and review cases where an injustice has been done to an injured employee, and the one-year statute of limitations does not apply to approved settlements.
