classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Review of Awards and Lump Sum Settlements
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act from 1927 outlines the procedures for reviewing awards and lump sum settlements. It clarifies that mutual agreements for lump sum settlements, once approved, are treated like awards made after hearings and can be set aside under specific conditions. It also states that the full payment of an award before the maximum compensation period expires does not prevent the plaintiff from having their case reopened. The process for determining the compensable period upon reopening an award is also detailed, emphasizing that only time during which compensation was paid under the original award is deducted, not intervening periods where no compensation was paid. Finally, it clarifies that awards made on review cannot be retroactive, preserving the integrity of prior awards until an application for review is made.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Alien Dependents
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines provisions for alien dependents of injured or deceased workers. It states that alien widows and children not residing in the United States will receive half the compensation of residents. Alien widowers, parents, brothers, and sisters not residing in the U.S. are not entitled to any compensation. The employer can commute future installments for alien dependents to their then value. The act also discusses the complexities of settlements with alien dependents and mentions a proposed but unenacted provision for payment to consular representatives.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses compensation for alien dependents. While it differentiates based on residency and relationship to the deceased worker, it does not mention race or create distinctions based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The category is 'labor' because it pertains to workmen's compensation. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the reference to 'Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 22, p. 369'.

upon orders. Operations also depend upon weather condi-
tions. * * * The price paid miners is regulated by what is
calleda 'scale' made between the operatorsand the union, and
one of the things always taken into considerationin fixingthe
wages of miners in thisdistrictis that the mine does not run
steadilyand the miner can only work when it does run."

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statutes - Lump Sum Payments
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which workers' compensation payments can be commuted to a lump sum. It specifies that this can occur after compensation has been paid for at least six months, upon application by either party and notice to the other. The Board must determine that it is in the best interest of either party and will not subject the employer or insurer to undue risk of overpayment. The lump sum amount is calculated based on the total sum of probable future payments, discounted at 5% per annum. Upon payment of the lump sum, all liability for the commuted payments ceases. It also mentions the appointment of a trustee to administer such lump sum payments.

classification.title: WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines the conditions under which compensation payments can be made in a lump sum. It specifies that lump sum payments are only possible after compensation has been paid for at least six months, and require an order from the Board after proper notice and a hearing. The employer is allowed a 5% annual discount on future payments. The Act also mentions that one of its purposes is to provide a regular income for injured workmen and their families, and that Section 26 is intended to protect them from squandering money. It also notes an exception for 'alien dependents' regarding lump sum payments.

Specifically, it states that if an employee intentionally fails to use a safety appliance or obey a lawful rule, their compensation may be reduced by 15%. However, this reduction does not apply if the employee's actions constitute 'deliberate intention' by the employer, 'wilful self-inflicted injury', 'wilful misconduct', or 'intoxication' on the part of the employee, which would bar them from benefits entirely. The text also references case law and annotations regarding employer liability and employee misconduct.

It clarifies that the intentional violation of a safety rule, such as riding on a loaded car in a coal mine against instructions, does not necessarily preclude recovery under the Act. Instead, it may lead to a reduction of compensation by 15 percent, as per Ky. Stats., sec. 4910.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the interpretation of 'wilful misconduct' within the context of Kentucky's Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It focuses on how intentional violations of safety rules affect an employee's compensation. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The category is 'labor' because it pertains to worker's compensation and employment regulations. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of an act from a specific legislative session.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Section 241
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act, specifically referencing Section 29 of the Act, outlines conditions under which an employer is entitled to a reduction in compensation payments. These conditions include the employee's intentional failure to obey a lawful and reasonable rule, order, or regulation for the safety of employees or the public, or the intentional failure to use a safety appliance furnished by the employer. The forfeiture provision is limited to safety measures and does not apply to orders intended solely for property protection. It also clarifies that such failure is not an absolute defense in all cases and that the disobedience must have contributed to the injury.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It details provisions related to the reduction of compensation payments due to an employee's intentional failure to follow safety rules or use safety equipment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on workplace safety and employee conduct. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The category is 'labor' as it directly pertains to employment and worker compensation. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the context of legislative acts from a specific year.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 - Section 29 Application
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act, as interpreted by court cases, outlines penalties for employee misconduct. Specifically, it addresses situations where an employee's failure to use provided safety appliances or obey rules can lead to a reduction in compensation by 15 percent. The text distinguishes this from 'wilful misconduct,' which could result in a total forfeiture of compensation. The intent of Section 29 is to encourage accident prevention through the use of safety measures for employees and the public, rather than to serve as a substitute for contributory negligence defenses or to address property preservation.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Section 29
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act, specifically Section 29, outlines conditions under which an employee's actions can affect their compensation. It distinguishes between risks incidental to employment and those outside of it. It also details penalties, including a 15% penalty, for intentional failure to obey lawful employer rules for safety, and notes that this does not exclude other forfeiture provisions. The section emphasizes that employer rules must be enforced or a reasonable attempt made to enforce them to be binding. A case example illustrates that an accident occurring when an employee performs a forbidden task is not considered to have arisen out of or in the course of employment.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It discusses the legal framework for determining an employee's eligibility for compensation based on their actions, adherence to employer rules, and the nature of workplace accidents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on labor law and employer-employee responsibilities. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation - Illegal Employment of Minors
classification.summary: This statute addresses the compensation for minors injured or killed while illegally employed. It outlines options for the minor's representative to claim compensation under the Workmen's Compensation Act or sue for damages. A proviso clarifies that if a minor under sixteen procures employment by misrepresenting their age with parental consent, they must rely solely on the Workmen's Compensation Act claim. The statute also defines 'wilful' in the context of statutes.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the employment of minors and their rights to compensation or damages in case of injury or death. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the citation format 'Acts 1916, Chap. 33' and 'Acts 1924, Chap. 70'.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Failure to Guard Dangerous Machinery
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act addresses the employer's responsibility to guard dangerous machinery. It clarifies that failure to guard machinery does not automatically render an infant's employment in willful violation of the Act, nor does it grant an election to sue at law for damages if both employer and employee have accepted the Act's provisions. It also states that if machinery cannot be guarded, the failure to guard it cannot be considered negligence.

It also addresses situations where the employer has knowledge of the injury, is not prejudiced by a lack of formal notice, or is misled. It also touches upon voluntary payments made by the employer and notice of a changed condition.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Notice Requirements
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the requirements for providing notice of injury to an employer. It specifies that prompt notice is necessary, and failure to do so can be prejudicial if it prevents the employer from investigating the cause of the injury, such as a tubercular condition. The statute also states that if notice is not an issue in a proceeding, the claimant is not required to prove it. Written notice must be given as soon as practicable unless the employer had knowledge or there was a reasonable cause for delay. Minor variances between the application and proof of injury are not fatal if they refer to the same occurrence, and the sufficiency of the application is not judged by strict pleading rules. Substantial compliance with procedural terms is sufficient.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation - Limitations of Claims and Notice of Accident
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statutes outlines the procedures and limitations for filing workmen's compensation claims. It specifies that claims for death benefits on behalf of minor children can be made by the widow and mother, who acts as their next friend, and that minority does not toll the statute of limitations. Claims must be filed within one year of the accident, and ignorance of the right of action does not prevent the statute from running. The statute also details the requirement for employees to provide notice of an accident or injury to the employer 'as soon as practicable,' emphasizing a liberal construction of this phrase to avoid defeating meritorious claims.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Statute of Limitations and Payments
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act from 1927 clarifies the statute of limitations for filing claims. It specifies that voluntary payments of compensation, as defined by the Act (weekly money payments), must be made within one year of the accident. Payments to a widow from a 'Subscriptions and Charities Fund' were not considered voluntary compensation. Similarly, voluntary payment of medical expenses by an insurance carrier does not toll the statute of limitations. The Act intends for 'payments of compensation' to mean weekly monetary payments to the employee. Giving expert medical attention in cases of doubtful liability should not create an estoppel against the employer, as this would submerge the humanitarian purposes of the Act in legal technicalities and could cause hardship to injured workers.

classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act, enacted in 1927, outlines the employer's right to request a physical examination of an injured employee. The employee also has the right to have their own physician present during the examination.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Physical Examination of Injured Employees
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rights and procedures for employers to require injured employees to undergo physical examinations. It specifies that these examinations must be conducted at reasonable times, places, and under reasonable conditions. The employer bears the cost of the examination, but the employee may have their own physician present. Refusal or obstruction of such examinations by the employee will result in the suspension of compensation payments for the period of non-compliance. The statute also clarifies that the Compensation Act aims to provide benefits for all injuries arising out of and in the course of employment, and the employer is responsible for medical treatment. The right to examine the employee is granted to prevent abuse and ascertain the extent and duration of injuries, and to check for malingering.

classification.title: LIMITATIONS AS TO MINORS AND MENTAL INCOMPETENTS
classification.summary: This statute, Section 273 of the Kentucky Law of 1927, addresses limitations on time for filing claims under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It states that these limitations will not run against individuals who are mentally incompetent or minor dependents, as long as they do not have a committee, guardian, next friend, or other authorized person to claim compensation on their behalf. The statute was amended in 1924 to include "other persons authorized to claim compensation for him under section 28 of this Act."

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Interpretation of Sections 28 and 38
classification.summary: This statute section interprets provisions of the Workmen's Compensation Act, specifically sections 28 and 38, concerning the appointment of a statutory next friend for minor dependents of deceased employees. It clarifies that if a dependent widow is the head of a family with minor children, she acts as the statutory next friend for her children. If dependents are not sui juris and the head of the family is not a dependent, then a committee, guardian, or next friend can make the application. The section also addresses the running of limitations periods for claims when dependents are not sui juris, stating that the limitation will not be suspended if they have a committee, guardian, or next friend.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an excerpt from the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. It discusses legal procedures for dependents of deceased employees to claim death benefits, focusing on the appointment of legal representatives for minors or those not legally competent (non sui juris). The text does not contain any mention of race, nor does it imply any racial distinctions in its application. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to an 'Act' and is presented as part of a compilation of statutes from a specific year.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Qualifications of Members of Board
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the qualifications for members of the Workmen's Compensation Board. It specifies that members must be at least thirty years of age, a resident of Kentucky for at least three consecutive years preceding their appointment, and of good moral character. It also states that no person accepting appointment and qualifying as a member shall be eligible for election or appointment to another position.

classification.title: Removal of Members of Board - Procedure
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for the removal of members of a Board by the Governor. Reasons for removal include inefficiency, neglect of duty, misconduct in office, political activity, or becoming ineligible. The Governor must provide advance notice of charges and an opportunity for a public hearing. A representative from the Attorney General's office shall attend and advise. The statute also allows for removal by the Senate through impeachment. The Governor must file a statement of charges, findings, and a record of proceedings with the Secretary of State.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the reference to 'Acts 1916, Chap. 33'. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines a procedural mechanism unrelated to race.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the provisions for the traveling expenses of the Workmen's Compensation Board and its employees, including allowances for transportation, meals, and lodging. It also details the requirements for the Board's main offices in Frankfort, the maintenance of records, and the jurisdiction of suits involving the Board in the Franklin Circuit Court.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Procedural Interpretation
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act from 1927 discusses the procedural aspects of claims. It emphasizes a simplified and prompt adjustment of claims, allowing for liberality in investigation but requiring that findings and awards be based on competent and relevant evidence. It defines 'summary manner' as a short, concise, and immediate proceeding and clarifies that the Act provides new machinery as a substitute for traditional courts to ensure speedy and scientific adjustment of compensation for industrial injuries. The courts are to construe the Act liberally to achieve its purpose, upholding substantial compliance with its terms.

classification.title: AGREEMENTS IN REGARD TO COMPENSATION
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Acts outlines the process for agreements and settlements regarding workers' compensation. It details how agreements between employers and employees (or their insurance companies) about compensation must be filed with and approved by the Compensation Board to be enforceable. It also clarifies that voluntary payments are permissible without formal agreement, but only a filed and approved memorandum of agreement or the expiration of a time limit constitutes a final settlement. The text also includes a note about the court disregarding errors that do not affect substantial rights and the Compensation Board's power to reopen cases based on changed conditions.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Claims and Awards
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act, specifically referencing Kentucky Statutes sections 4914, 4987, 4931, 4899, 4902, 4882, 4956, and 4957, details the procedures and timelines for making claims and reviewing awards. It clarifies that claims must be made within one year of the suspension of payments when compensation has been voluntarily paid, but this does not apply to payments under an agreed award. It also states that an agreement for compensation, when approved by the Compensation Board, becomes an award. Furthermore, it addresses the employer's right to waive an employee's failure to accept the Compensation Act's provisions before an accident and to agree with the employee for compensation after an accident.

classification.title: FINAL RELEASE (Workmen's Compensation)
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky statutes, concerning workmen's compensation, details the conditions under which a final release or settlement for an injury is considered binding. It states that a final release is not binding if the employee had not recovered from their injuries at the time of signing, especially if induced by a false medical report. Similarly, a physician's final report discharging an employee is not conclusive and can be contradicted by competent testimony. Agreements for compensation are not conclusive if they do not cover the correct amount justly due to the injured employee, and reliance on such agreements is not a valid defense under the Workmen's Compensation Act.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Agreements and Awards
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 details the conditions under which agreements made between employers and employees regarding compensation can be set aside. It specifies that applications to set aside agreements must be filed promptly after disagreement or cessation of payments, unless notice was given 'as soon as is practicable.' Agreements approved by the Board are legally binding like awards. To review or change a previous award or agreement, there must be a showing of a change in conditions, mistake, or fraud. The essentials leading up to an agreement are considered res judicata, except for those concerning the employee's physical condition.

denying awards.

ina prompt,simplemanner withouttheaidofa jury;27 Am .&

with a statement of the findingsof fact,rulingsof law and any
other matters pertinentto the question at issueshallbe filed
with the recordof proceedings" (seesec.299,supra),and a copy
of the award shallbe sentto each of the parties.

absence of a petitionfora reviewby thefullBoard afterthe lapse

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Evidence Standards
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the standards for using circumstantial evidence to justify awards. It states that circumstantial evidence is sufficient if it allows for a fair inference of liability without resorting to mere guess or conjecture. The Act requires competent and relevant evidence, whether direct or circumstantial, for findings and awards. While compensation is allowed for disability established by circumstantial evidence, speculative opinions are not grounds for an allowance. The burden of proof rests on the plaintiff and can be met with circumstantial evidence or inferences with a substantial basis. Actionable negligence and agency can also be established by circumstantial evidence, provided the circumstances themselves are proven and not presumed.

classification.title: EVIDENCE - RES GESTAE
classification.summary: This statute defines and explains the legal concept of 'res gestae' in Kentucky law, specifically concerning the admissibility of declarations and acts as evidence. It clarifies that for a declaration to be part of the res gestae, it must be made by an actor in the transaction, be contemporaneous with the event, made at or near the place of occurrence, and explanatory of the main fact. Statements made by third parties or bystanders, or those made long after the event, are generally not admissible as part of the res gestae.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the legal principle of 'res gestae' which pertains to the admissibility of evidence in legal proceedings. It outlines the conditions under which statements or actions can be considered part of the 'res gestae' (things done when the main transaction occurs and contemporaneous with its doing). The text focuses on the timing, location, and speaker of declarations to determine their admissibility. classification.title: Workmen's Compensation - Evidence - Speculation or Conjecture
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation statute addresses the admissibility of evidence and the prohibition of speculation in determining liability. It clarifies that hearsay statements made to attending physicians are admissible as res gestae. It also states that a foreman's testimony about hammer marks found on a gear wheel after an accident was admissible. Furthermore, the statute prohibits recovery based on speculation when multiple theories exist, one fixing liability and another not. It also states that an award cannot be based on a conjectural theory if the proximate cause of disability is not established, and that the cause of death or disability must not be left to speculation. If the cause or inception date of disability is not sufficiently shown, the Board cannot speculate between theories that would require an award and those that would not.

§ 320 WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION [§ 52 OF ACT

*provided, or an award or order of the Board upon review as provided in section 51, shall be conclusive, and binding as to all questions of fact, but either party may, within twenty days after the rendition of such final order or award of the Board, by petition appeal to the circuit court that would have jurisdiction to try an action for damages for said injuries if this Act had not passed, for the review of such order or award, the Board and the adverse party being made respondents. Such petition shall state fully the grounds upon which a review is sought, assign all errors relied on and shall be verified by the petitioner, who shall furnish copies of the petition to the respondents at the time of filing same.*

*Summons shall issue upon the petition, directing the adverse party to file answer within fifteen days after service thereof and directing the Board to certify its complete record of the case to the court, or, in lieu thereof, and if consented to by the petitioner and adverse party, an abstract of the record prepared in the same manner as hereinafter provided for appeals to the Court of Appeals.*

*No new or additional evidence may be introduced in the circuit court, except as to the fraud or misconduct of some person engaged in the administration of this Act, and affecting the order, ruling or award, but the court shall otherwise hear the cause upon the record or abstract thereof as certified by the Board, and shall dispose of the cause in summary manner, its review being limited to determining whether or not:*

1.  *The Board acted without or in excess of its powers.*
2.  *The order, decision or award was procured by fraud.*
3.  *The order, decision or award is not in conformity to the provisions of this Act.*
4.  *If findings of fact are in issue, whether such findings of fact support the order, decision or award.*

*The Board and each party shall have the right to appear in such review proceedings; the court shall enter judgment affirming, modifying or setting aside the order, decision or award, or in its discretion remanding the cause to the Board for further proceedings in conformity with the direction of the court. The court may, in advance of judgment and upon a sufficient showing of fact, remand the cause to the Board. (Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 52, p. 383.)*

It details how judgments are entered, including affirming, modifying, or setting aside awards, and the court's discretion in remanding cases. It also specifies that circuit courts cannot enter judgments on compromises without the consent of the Workmen's Compensation Board.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses legal procedures related to workmen's compensation appeals in Kentucky circuit courts. It focuses on the judicial process of reviewing and acting upon decisions made by the Workmen's Compensation Board. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code referencing previous statutes and case law. The confidence is high because the content is clearly procedural and unrelated to racial discrimination.

§§ 331-334 [§ 52 OF ACTWORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
SEC . 331. JUDGMENT OF CIRCUIT COURT CERTIFIED TO
COMPENSATION BOARD .
On appealfrom an award of Compensation Board , final
judgment by the circuitcourtshouldbe certifiedto the Board ,
but failureto do so,being a mere clericalerror,may be corrected.
Black Mountain Corporationv. Humphrey , 211 Ky . 533, 277
S. W. 833 .
For additionalauthorities,see American Digest (West Pub .
Co.),titleMaster& Servant,Key No. sec.417 (9).
SEC . 332. MOTION FOR NEW TRIAL .
In an action in the circuitcourt to set aside award of Work-
men's Compensation Board and for allowance of fullcompensa-
tion for death, where allegationsof petitionshowed that it was
not filedin time requiredby Ky .Stats., secs.4933-4935 , only a
questionof law was presentedby record,and no motion for new
trialwas necessary. Lena Rue Coal Co. v . Brewer, 213 Ky .
327 ,280 S. W. 1097 .
SEC . 333. AWARD CANNOT BE ATTACKED COLLATERALLY .
In an actionby dependentsto enforcean award ofcompen-
sationforthe death of an employe the employercannot setup
the defensethatthe award was voidforwant ofjurisdictionin
the Board to allow compensation, because the accident which
caused death did not ariseout of the employment ; the claim
beingthatthe Board made an honestmistakeof law (that is,
of undisputedfacts), the employer'sremedy being by appeal.
Ashland Iron & Mining Co. v .McDaniel ,202 Ky .19, 258 S. W.
943 .
For additionalauthorities,seeAmerican Digest(West Pub .
Co.),titleMaster & Servant,Key No. sec.416.
SEC . 334. FINDINGS OF LAW NOT CONCLUSIVE .
(a) Findings of law of the Compensation Board are not
conclusive, but are reviewable on appeal to the circuitcourt.
Northeast Coal Co. v . Castle,202 Ky . 205, 260 S. W. 336 .

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Statutes
classification.summary: These sections of the 1927 Kentucky statutes outline procedures and legal principles related to workmen's compensation awards. They cover the certification of circuit court judgments to the Compensation Board, the necessity of motions for new trials, the inability to collaterally attack awards, and the reviewability of findings of law by the Compensation Board on appeal.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of workmen's compensation claims in Kentucky. It discusses the legal processes for appealing awards, the finality of judgments, and the review of legal findings. There is no mention of race, and the subject matter (workmen's compensation) does not inherently suggest a racial dimension. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Law - Judicial Review of Board Findings
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the conditions under which judicial review of the Compensation Board's findings is permitted. It specifies that when there are no issues of fact or when facts are undisputed, the Board's finding becomes a question of law, subject to court review. This includes findings on whether an accident arose out of employment, or whether a deceased individual was an employee or an independent contractor. The statute also states that if there is no evidence to support the Board's findings, or if the Board enters an award not authorized by law, such decisions are reviewable as questions of law.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the legal procedures for reviewing decisions made by the Workmen's Compensation Board. It focuses on the distinction between findings of fact and findings of law, and the circumstances under which courts can review these findings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it appears to be part of a compiled body of statutes, likely the Kentucky Revised Statutes or a similar codification, given the section numbering and the reference to prior case law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses legal procedure without any racial implications.

classification.title: SEC. 335. FINDINGS OF FACT CONCLUSIVE.
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines the conclusiveness of findings of fact made by the Workmen's Compensation Board. It states that if these findings are supported by competent, credible, or substantial evidence, and there is no misapplication of the law, they are binding and conclusive upon the courts. This applies to all awards or orders of the Board in the absence of fraud.

classification.title: Conclusiveness of Board, commission or trial court's findings in proceedings under Compensation Acts.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for the conclusiveness of findings of fact made by the Compensation Board in proceedings under Compensation Acts. It states that these findings are to be treated similarly to a jury's verdict and will not be disturbed by a court unless clearly against the weight of the evidence, or if there is an entire absence of evidence to support them. Several case citations are provided to support these principles.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the legal standards for reviewing findings of fact made by a Workmen's Compensation Board. It focuses on the weight of evidence and the conditions under which a court can overturn such findings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to sections of an Act and cites previous case law, suggesting it is part of a compiled legal code or statute book rather than a standalone session law. The category is 'labor' as it directly pertains to workmen's compensation.

It outlines the conditions under which the Workmen's Compensation Board's findings of fact would not be disturbed by the circuit court or the Court of Appeals. It references specific case law to support these principles.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Judicial Review of Awards
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the conditions under which courts can review and set aside awards made by the Compensation Board. It specifies that courts cannot interfere if there is substantial or credible evidence to support the board's findings of fact. Hearsay evidence alone is insufficient to support an award, but its admission is not prejudicial if there is other competent evidence. The Board is not bound by strict technical rules of evidence but must observe fundamental principles of judicial inquiry. Awards not authorized by law, even if based on admitted facts, are reviewable as questions of law. Findings of fact by the Board are conclusive if supported by substantial evidence and there is no misapplication of the law.

It focuses on the admissibility and sufficiency of evidence, the distinction between questions of law and fact, and the conclusiveness of the board's findings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. § 336 WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION [§§ 53–55 OF ACT

**SEC. 336.** (SEC. 53 OF THE ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4936.)

**APPEALS TO COURT OF APPEALS.** *Where an amount sufficient under existing laws to authorize an appeal to the Court of Appeals is involved, the judgment of the circuit court shall be subject to appeal to the Court of Appeals, the scope of whose review shall include all matters herein made the subject of review by the circuit court and also errors of law arising in the circuit court and upon appeal made reviewable by the Civil Code of Procedure where not in conflict with the provisions of this Act.* The procedure as to appeal to the Court of Appeals shall be the same as in civil actions, so far as the same may be applicable to and not in conflict with the provisions of this Act, except as follows:

1.  *Only so much of the evidence shall be embraced in a bill of exceptions as may be necessary to present clearly the questions of law involved in the rulings to which exceptions are reserved, and such evidence as is embraced therein shall be set forth in condensed and narrative form, save as a proper understanding of the questions presented may require that parts of it be set forth otherwise.*

2.  *In order to carry out the provisions of subsection 1 and further to reduce the size of the record on appeal, it shall be the duty of the appellant to file with the clerk of the circuit court, together with proof or acknowledgment of service of a copy on the appellee or his counsel, a schedule which shall indicate the portions of the record to be incorporated into the transcript of the record on such appeal. Should the appellee or his counsel desire additional portions of the record incorporated into the record to be filed in the Court of Appeals, he may file with the clerk of the circuit court his schedule also, within ten days thereafter (unless the term be extended by order of the circuit court or of the Court of Appeals), indicating such additional portions of the record desired by him.*

*The clerk of the circuit court shall transmit to the Court of Appeals as the transcript of the record only those portions of the record in the lower court which are designated by the parties as above provided. The parties, or their counsel, may, however, agree by written stipulation to be filed with the clerk of the circuit court, the portions of the record which shall constitute the transcript of record on appeal, and the clerk in such case shall transmit only the*

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Enforcement of Agreements or Awards
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the process by which circuit courts can enforce agreements or awards made by the Workmen's Compensation Board. It details how a party can file a certified copy of an agreement, order, decision, or award with the circuit court, which will then render a judgment. The judgment has the same effect as one rendered in a regular court case. The section also addresses how judgments can be modified to conform to subsequent decisions of the Board regarding weekly payments.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the enforcement of workmen's compensation awards and agreements. It details the legal procedures for making these awards legally binding through circuit court judgments. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply generally to all parties involved in workmen's compensation claims. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the reference to 'Acts 1916, Chap. 33, sec. 56, p. 387' and 'Acts 1926, chap. 193'.

It details how approved agreements can be filed in circuit court for enforcement if a party fails to comply. It also states that awards or orders from the Board are enforceable similarly, but only after the time for review or appeal has expired. classification.summary: This statute, Section 362 of the 1927 Kentucky Law of Kentucky, outlines the insurance requirements for employers under the Workmen's Compensation Act. Employers must either insure their liability for compensation with an authorized insurance corporation or demonstrate financial ability to pay compensation directly. If demonstrating financial ability, a security, indemnity, or bond may be required. Employers must file evidence of compliance with the Board. Failure to comply makes the employer liable for compensation under the Act or at law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Law - Insurance Carriers
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Law from 1927 discusses the liabilities and rights of insurance carriers. It covers situations where an insurer might be estopped from denying coverage, the scope of an insurance carrier's liability for injuries not arising out of employment, subrogation rights, and the binding nature of findings upon claims for compensation. It also touches upon an insurer's right to recover from an employer who breaches a warranty.

It details the responsibilities and rights of insurance carriers in relation to employers and employees. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices based on race. Stats., sec. 4946) and legal precedents, suggesting it's part of a compiled legal code.

classification.title: SEC. 368. CARRYING OWN RISK REVOCATION OF PRIVILEGE - PARTIAL INSURANCE.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions and procedures for employers to carry their own workers' compensation risk in Kentucky. It details requirements for proving financial ability, depositing securities or surety bonds to guarantee payments, and obtaining a certificate from the Board. The Board has the authority to revoke this certificate with notice and a hearing, and reinstatement is not guaranteed within six months of revocation. Employers can also reinsure all or part of their risk, with the Board having no jurisdiction over the character or manner of reinsurance if the employer carries their entire risk. The statute notes that courts recognize a majority of employers insure their liability.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the regulations for employers carrying their own workers' compensation risk. It details financial requirements, the role of the Board, and the process for revoking or reinstating the privilege to self-insure. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The category is 'labor' as it pertains to employment and worker compensation. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the context of historical statutes and the page numbering. Confidence is high as the content is clearly about insurance and employer obligations, with no racial element.

entries.ocr_text:
§ 369
WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
[§ 65 OF ACT

## THE ACT, SECTION 65

### KINDS OF INSURANCE AUTHORIZED

**SEC. 369.** (SEC. 65 OF THE ACT.) KINDS OF INSURANCE AUTHORIZED.
**SEC. 370.** INSURANCE IN MUTUAL ASSOCIATIONS—RECIPROCAL OR INTER-INSURANCE EXCHANGES.

**SEC. 369.** (SEC. 65 OF THE ACT; KY. STATS., SEC. 4948.) **KINDS OF INSURANCE AUTHORIZED.** *For the purpose of complying with the provisions of section 63 groups of employers are hereby authorized to form either among themselves or with employers in other states mutual insurance associations or reciprocal or inter insurance exchanges subject to the general laws of this State relating to such mutual insurance associations or reciprocal or inter-insurance exchanges and such reasonable conditions and restrictions not inconsistent therewith as may be fixed by the Board.* Membership in such mutual insurance associations or reciprocal or inter-insurance exchanges so approved, together with evidence of the payment of premiums of payment due, shall be evidence of compliance with section 63. *The Board shall have power in any case to require any mutual insurance association or reciprocal or inter-insurance exchange to purchase an annuity or to effect reinsurance with a company authorized to transact insurance in this State or to make such deposit with a bank or trust company of this State as shall in either case be approved by said Board for the purpose of fully securing the payment of all deferred installments upon any claim for compensation.* *Provided, however, that any mutual insurance association or reciprocal or inter-insurance exchange possessing a surplus of at least one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) and not less in amount than the capital required of a Domestic Stock Insurance Company transacting the same kind of insurance, shall not be required to purchase an annuity or effect reinsurance with a company authorized to transact insurance in this State or to make such a deposit with a bank or trust company of this State for the purpose of fully securing the payment of all deferred installments upon any claim for compensation.*

classification.title: INSURANCE - DIRECT LIABILITY TO EMPLOYE
classification.summary: This statute, Section 375 of the Acts of 1916, Chapter 33, outlines the requirements for insurance policies against liability for compensation under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It mandates that policies must include an agreement by the insurer to promptly pay all benefits and compensation awarded or agreed upon. The insurer's obligations are not affected by any default in notice or otherwise, and the agreement is construed as a direct promise to the person entitled to compensation, enforceable in their name. Section 376 further states that employees are made direct parties in interest.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Regulation of Insurance Rates
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927 outlines the requirements for insurance policies and the regulation of insurance rates. It mandates that policy forms must be filed with and approved by the Commissioner of Insurance at least thirty days prior to issuance, unless approved sooner. The Commissioner can reject forms that do not comply with state laws, with a right to appeal to the Franklin Circuit Court. It also states that insurance rates charged by carriers must be fair and reasonable.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Insurance Rates
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Acts details the regulation of insurance rates for workmen's compensation policies. It mandates that rates must be fair, reasonable, and adequate, with due allowance for merit rating. All risks of the same kind and degree of hazard must be written at the same rate by the same carrier. Policies are invalid until rates are approved by the Board, and carriers must file their rating schedules. Insurance carriers are required to report their financial condition to the State Insurance Commissioner to determine solvency and rate adequacy. The Board or Commissioner has the power to inspect books and records and examine officers under oath. A court case is cited regarding the presumption of approval for rates paid and acquiesced in by the insured.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of insurance rates for workmen's compensation. It outlines requirements for fairness, adequacy, and approval of these rates by a state board and commissioner. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is purely on the financial and regulatory aspects of insurance for worker compensation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

An election for a particular business, such as a junk dealer, does not automatically cover other operations like building wrecking.

classification.title: Acceptance of Act by Employe
classification.summary: This section of the 1927 Kentucky Acts outlines the process by which an employee can elect to operate under the provisions of the Kentucky Workmen's Compensation Act of 1916. It specifies that acceptance is effected by signing a notice agreeing to the Act's provisions. The election is effective from the date of signing. For employees unable to write, their mark must be witnessed by a third person who reads the notice to them. Multiple employees can sign the same notice, provided a copy of the notice is at the top of each page with signatures. The section also addresses situations where employment is temporarily suspended.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Acceptance by Employee
classification.summary: This section of the Workmen's Compensation Act outlines the process for employees to accept the act. After an employer elects to accept the act, employees must be notified and given an opportunity to agree to operate under its provisions. The agreement must be in a prescribed form, with the date of signing recorded. Acceptance remains effective as long as the employee remains with the same employer, unless withdrawn according to section 76. Even with employment suspensions of less than a year, the original acceptance continues unless withdrawn. The statute also notes amendments made in 1922 and 1926.

This ruling aims to prevent hardship and ensure the Workmen's Compensation Act remains operative.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Withdrawal of Acceptance
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for employers and employees to withdraw their acceptance of the Workmen's Compensation Act. It details the requirements for filing written notices, the effective dates of withdrawal, and the posting of notices at the place of business. It also clarifies that the death of an employee or a suit by an administrator does not constitute a withdrawal, and that withdrawal is not effective for injuries sustained less than one week after filing the notice.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Withdrawal from Election
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for employers and employees to withdraw from the Workmen's Compensation Act. It details the notice requirements for employers, including personal written notice or posting in conspicuous places, with a minimum one-week notice period. Employees can withdraw by filing written notice with the employer, also with a one-week minimum effective date. classification.summary: This statute clarifies aspects of the Workmen's Compensation Act concerning the death of an employee or changes in company ownership. It states that an employee's election to operate under the Act is not discharged by their death or by their administrator suing for damages. It also addresses that the death of an employee who had elected to operate under the Act does not rescind their election. Furthermore, it clarifies that an employee's personal representative has no vested right in a cause of action for death protected by the Constitution if the employee had elected to be bound by the Workmen's Compensation Act. The statute also touches upon withdrawal by an employer and changes in company ownership.

classification.title: SEC. 411. RIGHT OF LEGISLATURE TO ABROGATE COMMON-LAW DEFENSES.
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky statutes, enacted in 1927, discusses the validity of the Compensation Act of 1916. It clarifies that the Act is not invalid for abolishing common-law defenses such as fellow servant, assumed risk, and contributory negligence in certain cases, or for allowing these defenses in others. It also states that elective Workmen's Compensation Acts take away these common-law defenses for employers who come within their purview and fail to elect to accept the Acts, and that these provisions have been uniformly upheld as the legislature has the power to abolish such rules of law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Restoration of Defenses
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which an employer can restore common law defenses (contributory negligence, negligence of a fellow servant, and assumption of risk) in lawsuits for personal injury or death. This occurs if an employee affected by the Workmen's Compensation Act chooses not to operate under its provisions, or if the employer elects to operate under the Act and the employee rejects it. In such cases, the legal proceedings revert to the common law as if the Act had not been enacted.

classification.title: ANNUAL REPORT OF THE BOARD
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for the Compensation Board to submit an annual report to the Governor by December 15th. The report must include statistics on awards and rejected claims, causes of accidents, financial statements of the maintenance fund, and any other information the Board deems relevant. The Board is also tasked with publishing and distributing general information about its business to employers and employees, with a caveat that abuse of this right constitutes political activity. The report is limited to five hundred copies, and all printing is to be done by the state's public printing contractor.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative reporting requirements for a Compensation Board. It details the content and distribution of an annual report and mentions the process for public printing. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details a 2% tax on premiums received by insurance carriers insuring employers against liability for employee injuries or death. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Maintenance Fund Tax
classification.summary: This statute outlines the provisions for employers who choose to carry their own risk under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It details the requirement for these employers to report their payroll, the assessment of a maintenance fund tax (2% of basic premiums), and conditions under which this tax may be waived or redirected to the State Treasury if the maintenance fund surplus exceeds $60,000. It also references previous amendments and a court case related to these provisions.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Acceptance by Employee
classification.summary: This text discusses the conditions under which an employee's acceptance of the Workmen's Compensation Act is binding. It clarifies that the execution of the notice of election is an actual acceptance, not an offer, and remains binding until withdrawn. The death of an employee or their administrator suing for damages does not discharge this contract. If an employee returns to work within nine weeks of quitting or being discharged, their original acceptance is still binding unless withdrawn. The text also notes that an employer cannot avoid compensation if an employee signs a document on the wrong page at a foreman's direction, and that a minor employee is not bound by the Act at the time of acceptance or injury.

classification.title: Workmen's Compensation Act - Appeals Process
classification.summary: This document excerpt pertains to the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927, specifically detailing aspects of appeals to the circuit court. It outlines the conditions under which an employee is entitled to compensation, emphasizing that the hazard of employment acting upon an employee's existing health condition determines compensation rights, not the hazard's effect on a healthy individual. It also addresses the Board's errors in refusing to consider an employee's failure to follow medical advice and in finding that an accident was the approximate cause of death without sufficient evidence. The text also provides procedural information regarding appeals, including deadlines and parties involved.

There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. APPENDIX
FORM 44. Award .
FINDINGS OF FACT
The Board finds that the deceased, Will Smith's death arose out of,
and in the courseof hisemployment ,and thatthe plaintiff, Malvary Smith,
isentitledto compensationat $12.00per week for a periodof 335 weeks,
with 6% intereston allback payments due and for burial expensesnot to
exceed the sum of $ 75.00 .
RULING OF LAW
An employe whileactingas deputy sheriffand paid forhis servicesby
the defendantto keep peaceand orderon the premisesof the defendantand
iskilledwhen in performanceof hisduty, same isheld to be compensable
withinthe meaning of the Act,as his death aroseout of,and in the course
ofhisemployment by thedefendant.
AWARD
The plaintiff, Malvary Smith,isawarded compensationat the rateof
$12.00per week fora periodof 335 weeks,with 6 % intereston allback pay-
ments due and burialexpenses not to exceed the sum of $75.00.
Section50 ofthe Act providesthat"theBoard,orany ofits
members shallhear the partiesat issueand theirrepresentatives
and witnesses, and shalldetermine the dispute in a summary
manner . The award , togetherwith a statement of the findings
of fact, rulingsof law and any other matters pertinentto the
questionsat issueshallbe filedwith the recordof proceedings,
and a copy of the award shallimmediately be sent to the parties
in dispute." See alsoas to awards secs.306 and 307 ofthisbook .
The language of an award willbe construed liberallyand
not technically. A case should not be reversed on account of
technicalconstructionof the languageused by the Board in
making itsaward. Ashland LimestoneCo. v.Wright,219 Ky .
691 ,694 , 294 S. W. 159 .

classification.title: Report of Voluntary Commission on Workmen's Compensation
classification.summary: This document discusses the constitutionality of a 1914 workmen's compensation law in relation to Section 54 of the Constitution, which limits legislative recoveries. The court found that the law's increase of employer defenses against employees who rejected the act rendered their waiver of unlimited recovery involuntary and invalid. The report suggests that future laws must ensure employees retain their original cause of action and defenses if they refuse to accept the act. It also notes that most elective acts allow employers to retain common-law defenses if employees reject the act, while removing them if employers reject it. The text highlights that despite concerns about fairness to employees, statistics from other states show a negligible percentage of employees rejecting such laws, even with lower compensation rates. It anticipates that Kentucky employees will similarly accept a proposed law with a 65% compensation rate and more liberal benefits.

REASONS FOR THE ENACTMENT OF A COMPENSATION LAW
It has been customary in the past forreportsof commissions
to be prefacedby a more or lesslengthy and detailedexplanation
of the fundamental principlesof compensation as opposed to the
damage suitmethod of reimbursingsuch employesas might be
injuredunder circumstancespermittinglegalrecovery,and the
relativemerits of the two systems.
Public opinion, however, has progressedso rapidlyand
authenticinformationhas become so generalthat thisisno longer
necessary. The publichas become prettygenerallycognizantof
the factthat objectionto theselaws isnot emanating eitherfrom
the employe or employer classes, but from third parties. As a
resultof this, the spread of the compensation system has been
more rapidduringthelastfew yearsthan thatof any previous
economic reform in the historyof thiscountry. Thirty-two
statesand territories, comprising over 80 per cent of our total
population, are now (1915) under Compensation Laws, and in
twelve otherspreparationsare being made for theirenactment .
Startingin 1910, the New York law was passed;in 1911,
those of Illinois, Kansas , Massachusetts, New Hampshire , New
Jersey, Ohio , Washington , and Wisconsin; in 1912, Arizona,
Michigan , and Rhode Island; in 1913, California, Connecticut,
Iowa , Minnesota , Nebraska , Nevada , Oregon , Texas , West
Virginia, and the second New York act; in 1914, Kentucky,
Louisiana, and Maryland ; in 1915, Colorado, Indiana, Maine ,
Montana , Oklahoma , Pennsylvania, Vermont, Wyoming , and
the territoriesof Alaska and Hawaii . Other than the 1914
Kentucky Act,but two lawshad beenheldinvalidby thecourts.
In those states,New York and Montana ,subsequent actsmuch
more far-reaching than those which had been held unconstitu-
tional,were laterenacted and are now in force. In no statehas
a CompensationLaw ever been repealedand in the majority
lateramendments have widened their scope or changed them
from the electiveto the compulsory form .

entries.ocr_text:
# APPENDIX

rates. Such forms of insurance must, however, be self-supporting and run as solvent concerns furnishing full coverage and adequate service to the employe and employer.

The most successful solution of this problem has been the authorization of properly supervised mutuals and reciprocals, or inter-insurers, and a proper degree of supervision by the state of the rates of all insurers, both mutuals and stock companies, sufficient to insure solvency and prevent discrimination or unfair competition.

Massachusetts seems to have made more progress in the way of affording employers the benefits of competition in insurance and yet having its several forms of insurance carriers all solvent, self-supporting and operated on systems which will take care of the future as well as the present. As the Massachusetts law is nearing the close of its fourth year of successful operation which, taking into consideration its enormous aggregate pay roll of over $900,000,000 annually, gives an experience exceeding that of any other state, and since more accurate, systematic and comprehensive statistics have been collected under it than are anywhere else available, the commission believes that the fundamental principles developed and conclusions arrived at in Massachusetts can be accepted as the most reliable in existence at this time. Their value to the disinterested investigator is considerably greater than that of the reports and statistics of some other states which are more or less in the nature of carefully prepared defenses of systems which are operating under constant pressure of a dissatisfied constituency.

Our commission, composed of representatives of capital, labor and the legal department of the state administration, have become, in the course of their investigation of the subject, very strongly impressed with the fact that legislation so vitally affecting the interests of every employe and employer in the state should not be undertaken along experimental or theoretical lines. # APPENDIX

instances. This association, in the commission's opinion, affords every advantage that can be claimed for a state fund, without its numerous and serious disadvantages.

Further proof of the soundness of the "State Mutual" plan is that the state of Texas, whose industrial and geographical conditions are very dissimilar to those of Massachusetts, adopted these provisions of the law of the latter state practically verbatim and the Texas association also proved successful.

Private mutuals, under strict regulation and supervision of the Board and the insurance department, are also provided since there is a demand for these among some of the larger individual industries which have found it practicable to operate as separate organizations under methods peculiarly adapted to the conditions of their respective industries. The successful "Contractors' Mutual" of Massachusetts is an instance.

The final insurance option included is that of reciprocal or inter-indemnity insurance. While there are not available to the commission complete or reliable statistics on this form of insurance, the general adoption of it by the later Compensation Acts, and its endorsement by both the Indiana and Missouri commissions, is sufficient to warrant its consideration. These being unincorporated associations of individuals whose business is carried on by an attorney-in-fact acting under a several power of attorney from each member, the most immediate consideration seems to be the method of regulating the operations of this attorney-in-fact so as to insure the adoption of sound business methods and continued solvency of the insurance furnished. On this account, it appears advisable to limit the organization of these reciprocals to employers who are under the Kentucky Act and within the jurisdiction of its courts, rather than to permit membership in some organization whose attorney-in-fact, assets and place of business are elsewhere and the majority of whose members are non-residents of the State. This form of insurance appears to have been successful in Illinois and Kansas and is now being developed in Indiana, Oklahoma and elsewhere.

On account of the absence of any extended experience developed by reciprocal insurance under other Compensation

(NOTE - The Board now followsBeaver Dam Coal Co. v .
Hocker , 202 Ky . 398, 259 S. W. 1010, and determines average

Section 24 of the Act is as follows :
"Sec.24. Compensationshallbe computed at theaverage
weekly wage earned by employe at time of injuryreckoning
wages as earned,whileworkingat fulltime.
"(a) Iftheemploye,at thetime oftheinjury,isregularly

duringthe year and with largerregularwages,onlysuchhigher
gradeof work or occupation, ifthe same be not seasonal,shall

wages ."

which "average" earningswould be calculatedby different
employes, employersand insurers, which would resultin great
inequalitiesand discriminationsin differentcases, the Board
has, under itsauthorityto make rulesfor carryingout the pro-
visionsof the Act , prescribedthe followingrulesfor computing

"Average weekly earningsshallbe computed by dividing

"(a) If the employe ispaid by the year,takehisyearly

INSTRUCTIONS AND RULES OF BOARD
RULES OF PROCEDURE BEFORE THE WORKMEN'S
COMPENSATION BOARD
(NOTE - These rulesof procedurebeforethe Board have not
been revised since 1920, and are not followed closelyby the
Board .)
1. Location of Main Office. The main officeof the Work-
men's Compensation Board isin the CapitolBuildingat Frank-
fort, Kentucky. The officewillbe open continuouslyfrom
eight-thirtyo'clocka. m .untilfiveo'clockp. m . ofeach week
day exceptlegalholidays.
2. Meetings of the Board .- The Board willhold regular
meetings at its main officebeginning at the hour of eleven
o'clocka. m .on the firstand thirdTuesdays of each month for
the transactionof allbusiness. The said regularmeetings will
be adjournedfrom saiddays untilallbusinessistransacted.
The Board will remain at the officeor be available thereto on
said regularmeeting days from the convening hour untilfive
o'clockp .m . Meetings of the Board willbe held at such other
times and placesas may be necessaryto considerand transact
business,when calledby the chairman .
3. Forms to Be Furnished by the Board .-The Board will
prepareand furnishfreeof chargeallproperforms requiredby
theprovisionsof the"Workmen's CompensationAct," and by
theserules,and such forms must be used in allinstanceswhere
theyareprescribed;provided,however,thatwhere a writtenor
typewrittenform substantiallyfollowsthe printedform, the
same willbe accepted. The useofprivatelyprintedformscon-
forming to those adopted by the Board isauthorized.
4. Hearings. Every hearingupon a claim, held before
theBoard or one ofitsmembers ,shallbe conducted in a summary
manner . Opportunity willbe given to the partiesat issueto
introducewitnessesand to present,eitherinperson or by counsel,
the points at issue. Such hearingsshallbe conducted in such
manner as to ascertainthe substantialrightsof the partiesand
to determine fairlyand expeditiouslythe controversy,but the
Board or a member thereofshallnot be bound by common law
or statutoryrulesofevidenceorby technicalrulesofprocedure.

classification.title: Industrial Rehabilitation Act
classification.summary: This act establishes the Industrial Rehabilitation Act in Kentucky, outlining eligibility requirements for vocational rehabilitation services. Disabled persons must have resided in the state for at least one year or be residing there at the time of disability. It mandates cooperation between the State Board and the Workmen's Compensation Board to formulate a plan for administering the act, subject to gubernatorial approval. The act also details appropriations for fiscal years 1922, 1923, and 1924, funded by taxes on insurance carriers writing compensation insurance, with a stipulation that funds are not to be used by institutions for handicapped persons except for special training determined by the State Board. The State Treasurer is designated as the custodian of all moneys.

The eligibility criteria are based on residency and disability status, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to 'Acts 1922, chap. 66, p. 207'.
